sources.name: Wikipedia — Earthworm Jim
sources.notes: Genesis is the primary version. Confirms whip mechanic, plasma gun, level variety.

notes: Earthworm Jim was developed simultaneously for Genesis and SNES using a custom heterogeneous programming language.
Genesis is the primary reference platform — physics originated on Genesis, then ported to SNES.
Jim is a regular earthworm who found an ultra-high-tech indestructible super suit.
DEFINING COMBAT: Plasma gun (main ranged weapon) + worm-head whip (melee/grapple).
DEFINING TRAVERSAL: Worm head as grappling hook on certain hooks — Jim grabs and swings.
Multiple distinct level types: platformer, underwater, asteroid-dodging, escort mission.
Walk speed, jump values not precisely documented — estimated from gameplay observation.

walk_speed.notes: Approximate. Jim runs in his suit. VERIFY.

ground_movement.notes: Jim moves at a consistent speed in his suit.

jump.notes: Standard variable-height jump with air control. VERIFY.

plasma_gun.description: Jim's primary weapon. Fires plasma bolts that bounce off walls.
notes: Plasma bolts bounce off surfaces, allowing indirect hits around corners.
Unlimited ammo for standard plasma bolts.
Super Plasma: charged shot that deals more damage. VERIFY charge time.
The bouncing trajectory creates environmental interaction — use walls for indirect hits.

worm_whip.description: Jim uses his own worm head/body as a whip attack. Press B to extend head for melee.
use_cases: Hit objects Jim's gun can't reach
Grapple point interaction (grab hooks to swing)

grapple_hook.description: On designated hook points in levels, Jim can grab with his worm head and swing in a pendulum arc.
notes: Similar to Bionic Commando's grappling arm but not the primary movement mode.
Jim extends his head upward to grab the hook.
Swings on an arc and releases at desired point.
Used in specific level sections for crossing gaps.

level_types._note: Earthworm Jim has several distinct level modes that use different physics/mechanics.

for_pete_sake.description: Jim must escort Major Mucus through a level. If Mucus hits an enemy or falls too far behind, he panics.
for_pete_sake.notes: Escort mission creates stress through protection of a pathfinding NPC.

andy_asteroids.description: Jim rides a hamster (Snot) in an asteroid field dodging obstacles.
andy_asteroids.notes: Completely different physics — free-flying in an asteroid field.

level_5.notes: Unique one-off mechanic — not representative of overall game physics.

suit_mechanics.description: Jim's suit provides his strength, jump capability, and weapon holster.
suit_mechanics.notes: The suit is 'indestructible' — damage is absorbed but Jim can still be knocked around.

jim_standing.notes: Jim is tall (about 2.5 tiles) but his hitbox is relatively narrow. VERIFY.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Earthworm Jim's level variety (platformer → escort → asteroid dodge) across a single game predates the 'genre-shifting' design that would become common later. Each mode uses the same character but different physics contexts.

The bouncing plasma gun creates indirect combat opportunities not available in straight-line projectile games. Players learn to use room geometry as part of their attack strategy — 'bounce this shot off that wall to hit the enemy behind cover.'

The worm whip as both melee and grapple uses a single mechanic for two purposes — the grapple is a situational upgrade of the melee (same button, different context). This single-mechanic depth avoids controller complexity.

The escort mission (For Pete's Sake) is philosophically opposite to the rest of the game: instead of 'survive yourself,' you must 'keep someone else alive.' The stress shifts from personal survival to responsibility. This is a fundamentally different emotional register.

Jim's visual comedy (earthworm in robot suit) creates a tonal framework where absurdity is expected. The level design leans into this: enemies include Evil Queen Slug-For-A-Butt, Professor Monkey-For-A-Head, and Bob the Killer Goldfish. Design and tone are unified.
